description: Person floating in sea-surface distress vs. breaking wave whitecap. Both present as small pale ovals at 20-40m UAV altitude.

rules.rule: When a small pale oval is visible above the wave crests with uniform brightness across its full extent and a clean elliptical boundary without radial fading, classify as person_in_water.
preconditions: A small pale oval region is visible above the surrounding wave crests, approximately 15-25 cm apparent width
Brightness is approximately uniform across the full extent of the oval — the centre and edges have similar intensity, unlike a whitecap which fades from its bright crest radially outward
The oval boundary is clean and elliptical, without the irregular fringing or foam dispersal characteristic of a breaking wave
rules.rationale: A floating human head maintains its shape and reflectance uniformly because it is a solid object at constant elevation above the waterline. A whitecap is a transient foam structure whose brightness peaks at the crest and fades outward as the bubble layer thins. This brightness uniformity distinction is observable in a single RGB frame without temporal comparison.
rules.notes: Seed rule — replace with output from run_dd_session.py after first live session.

rules.rule: When a pale oval region above the waterline shows a subtle bilateral darkening at the 3 o'clock and 9 o'clock positions at the waterline boundary, classify as person_in_water.
preconditions: A pale oval region is visible above the surrounding sea surface
Subtle darkening is visible at both lateral edges of the oval just at the waterline — a V or arc shape extending from the lower sides of the oval into the water surface
The darkening is bilateral and approximately symmetric about the vertical axis of the oval
rules.rationale: The instinctive drowning response causes arms to press laterally outward and downward just below the waterline. Submerged arms create a shadow or diffuse reflection difference at the water surface flanking the head. Floating debris of similar size does not produce this bilateral flanking pattern.
rules.notes: Secondary rule. Lower precision than piw_001 at scout resolution. More reliable on commander tier with higher resolution camera.

rules.rule: When a pale oval region is visible above the waterline, maintains a stable elliptical profile across at least 8 consecutive frames, and shows uniform brightness without the crest-to-edge fading of a whitecap, classify as person_in_water.
preconditions: A pale oval region is visible above the surrounding sea surface
The oval maintains its approximate position, size, and shape across at least 8 consecutive frames (~16 seconds at 0.5fps thermal pass)
Brightness is approximately uniform across the full oval extent — no crest fading
The oval does not disperse or break up into irregular foam over the observation window
rules.rationale: A whitecap blooms and disperses within 2-4 seconds. A floating human head maintains its position and profile across 10-20+ seconds. The commander tier's onboard frame buffer makes this temporal criterion directly observable. Combined with the single-frame brightness uniformity criterion, this achieves higher precision than the scout-tier rule alone.
rules.notes: Commander-tier rule. Includes temporal criterion (stable across frames) not available to single-frame scout classifiers.

rules.rule: When a small cluster of high-contrast objects in open water includes sub-meter bright spots with bilateral symmetry, adjacent saturated-color patches (red/orange), and nearby dark sub-pixel dots at body-length spacing, classify as person_in_water.
preconditions: Two or more bright white patches (high pixel intensity, near-saturated) appear in close spatial proximity against a uniform dark teal-green sea surface, each patch subtending roughly 0.5–1.5 m equivalent at estimated altitude

At least one bright patch exhibits bilateral symmetry about its long axis, consistent with a human torso/shoulders viewed from above — roughly oval or T-shaped rather than the irregular, elongated streak of a whitecap

Vivid red or red-orange color patches are co-located with or immediately adjacent to the white bright spots, consistent with PFD (personal flotation device) or rescue marker panel coloring — whitecaps are achromatic (white only) and never exhibit saturated red hues
Multiple small dark or orange-tinted dots are visible at 1–3 body-length spacing around the primary bright objects, consistent with heads of additional persons in water or deployed rescue markers
The bright patches lack the elongated, wind-aligned streaking and feathered trailing edge characteristic of true whitecaps; instead they are compact, roughly equi-axial blobs with defined edges

The surrounding water surface shows low sea-state texture (small capillary ripples, no organized wave crests), making true whitecap formation at this wind speed implausible — bright objects are anomalous against the ambient surface

The spatial arrangement of objects forms a loose cluster spanning roughly 20–40 m, consistent with a group of persons in water drifting together rather than a random distribution of wave features across the entire frame
rules.rationale: The scout classifier likely triggered on high-brightness white patches against the dark sea, which superficially resemble whitecaps. However, several single-frame optical features distinguish these objects: (1) the bright patches carry co-located saturated red/orange color that whitecaps never produce — diagnostic of PFDs, rescue dye, or safety-colored gear; (2) the shapes are compact and bilaterally symmetric rather than elongated wind-streaked arcs; (3) the ambient sea state shows only low capillary ripple with no other whitecaps anywhere, meaning Beaufort conditions are too calm for whitecap generation; (4) tiny dark dots at regular human-body-length spacing match the silhouette of partially-submerged heads; (5) tight spatial clustering of multiple distinct objects is consistent with persons grouped in water rather than stochastic wave breaking.
rules.confirmation_summary: Commander drone C2 FLIR confirmed persistent 37°C oval heat signature, stable across 30-second window, no thermal dispersion
rules.notes: First real DD session against SeaDronesSee val split. Pool: 10 positive (hardest, min person_fraction ≈0), 20 negative frames. Rule fires on multi-person group-in-water scenes with PFD/safety color. Lower recall (0.50) because many positive frames show single isolated swimmers without PFD color — a second session with single-swimmer frames should yield a complementary rule.

rules.rule: When a small cluster of high-contrast objects in open water includes sub-meter bright spots with bilateral symmetry, adjacent saturated-color patches (red/orange), and nearby dark sub-pixel dots at body-length spacing, classify as person_in_water.
preconditions: Two or more bright white patches (high pixel intensity, near-saturated) appear in close spatial proximity against a uniform dark teal-green sea surface, each patch subtending roughly 0.5–1.5 m equivalent at estimated altitude

At least one bright patch exhibits bilateral symmetry about its long axis, consistent with a human torso/shoulders viewed from above — roughly oval or T-shaped rather than the irregular, elongated streak of a whitecap

Vivid red or red-orange color patches are co-located with or immediately adjacent to the white bright spots, consistent with PFD (personal flotation device) or rescue marker panel coloring — whitecaps are achromatic (white only) and never exhibit saturated red hues
Multiple small dark or orange-tinted dots are visible at 1–3 body-length spacing around the primary bright objects, consistent with heads of additional persons in water or deployed rescue markers
The bright patches lack the elongated, wind-aligned streaking and feathered trailing edge characteristic of true whitecaps; instead they are compact, roughly equi-axial blobs with defined edges

The surrounding water surface shows low sea-state texture (small capillary ripples, no organized wave crests), making true whitecap formation at this wind speed implausible — bright objects are anomalous against the ambient surface

The spatial arrangement of objects forms a loose cluster spanning roughly 20–40 m, consistent with a group of persons in water drifting together rather than a random distribution of wave features across the entire frame
rules.rationale: Identical rule to piw_004 (scout tier). No tier-specific adaptation was needed: all 7 preconditions are single-frame observable at both scout (1–2 cm/px) and commander (0.5–1 cm/px) resolutions. The commander tier’s higher resolution and stabilised gimbal make the bilateral symmetry and PFD color accents even more reliable. The temporal grounding check classified zero preconditions as requiring multi-frame comparison.
rules.confirmation_summary: Commander drone C2 FLIR confirmed persistent 37°C oval heat signature, stable across 30-second window, no thermal dispersion
rules.notes: Commander-tier version of piw_004. Unlike the seed commander rule (piw_003) which required the temporal stability criterion (8+ frames), this rule is fully single-frame. Complements piw_003 by covering the multi-person group scenario.

confusable_pair_notes.primary_challenge: Both present as small pale ovals at 20-40m UAV altitude in moderate sea state. The classifier trained on normal swimming correctly classifies active swimmers but has no representation of the instinctive drowning response.
confusable_pair_notes.instinctive_drowning_response: Body near-vertical, head tilted back to keep mouth at waterline, arms pressing laterally outward and downward rather than stroking. Minimal forward movement. From above: a small pale oval with subtle bilateral disturbance at the waterline.
confusable_pair_notes.whitecap_signature: Bright at wave crest, fades radially to match surrounding water. Irregular boundary. Present for 2-4 seconds then disperses. No bilateral flanking pattern.
key_discriminators: Boundary geometry: clean ellipse (person) vs irregular foam fringe (whitecap)
Temporal stability: 10-20s+ (person) vs 2-4s (whitecap) — commander tier only
confusable_pair_notes.resolution_notes: At scout tier (1-2 cm/pixel at 30m, no stabilisation), bilateral arm darkening is marginal and removed from scout rule. Commander tier (0.5-1 cm/pixel, stabilised gimbal) can reliably observe it.
